name: Coinage Standards
notes: Standard currency denominations and their conversions. By extension: 1 gp = 10 sp = 100 bp = 1,000 cp.

name: Equipment Breakage
notes: Roll d100 (open-ended), adding the item's Strength (ST = inherent durability) and Quality/material modifier (Qual), and subtracting all damage penalties from previous checks (Dam). The Ammo column gives the percentage of ammunition that survives. UM 66 = an Unusual Event on an unmodified roll of 66.
rows: Hopefully, this wasn't a keepsake, or wanted in any way, because it is broken. Did you come prepared with a backup?
Even quality items cannot take such punishment over and over again. The item has been damaged and is at -20 until repaired.

Perhaps it's weird lighting, or your growing senility, but the item is not as damaged as you believed it to be. If a failure, it turns out the damage is only temporary. If a success, the item has miraculously been repaired of prior damage (reduce damage to item by up to 10).
The item was either hit in the right spot, or the creator cut some corners in the durability department. In any case the item is at -10 until it is repaired.
What a finely crafted item this is. Despite the beating that it receives it holds steady and true. If you are religious, this is a good time to thank your patron.
This item must be made of the FINEST MATERIALS KNOWN TO MANKIND! It appears completely impervious to damage. Maybe it's magic! Maybe it's been blessed! Or maybe the damage was just paltry.

name: Equipment Repair
notes: Repairing requires an appropriate crafting skill and proper materials, resolved as an Absolute Maneuver (the GM sets difficulty by the materials and tools at hand; the item's existing damage penalty applies to the roll). UM 66 = an Unusual Event on an unmodified roll of 66.
rows: Due to your ineptitude, the item breaks. If it was already broken, then you make a huge mess and the item is unrepairable. And everyone laughs at you.
Despite, or perhaps because of, your efforts, the item remains broken or damaged. You are probably better off buying a new one, hope it wasn't sentimental.

You're a clever one (you think). You don't manage to repair the item exactly, but rather jury-rig it, which is then either functional (Partial Success or higher) or completely non-functional (Failure or less). Either way it looks makeshift and kind of stupid.
Either your skill is lacking or your heart wasn't into it. A completely broken item cannot be repaired, although any damage penalties may be reduced by half.
A thorough, complete and solid piece of work. You completely repair a damaged item, or fix a broken item (with a permanent -10 penalty).
You are a repair-wizard! You manage to completely repair a damaged or broken item as good as new. Too bad your master-craft skills are not being used to make new unique works.

name: General Equipment
notes: Common gear and goods. Cost is in standard coinage (gp/sp/bp/cp). Prod Time is the time to craft the item. Strength is the item's inherent durability used in breakage checks. A dash (-) means the value does not apply.
rows: Leather or canvas. Holds 20 lbs; 1 cu'.
Wooden shaft with hair bristles, capped
Gives 2x advantage on feats of strength
Gives 1.5x advantage on feats of strength
Musical instrument, e.g. lute, lyre, viol.
Includes 1 pt oil (6 hr lantern refill)
Iron, two keys, medium difficulty to open
Iron, two keys, hard difficulty to open
One week's food for one person
One week's preserved food for one person

name: Armor
notes: Table 6-4 (armor portion). Weights assume an average human of 185 lbs. Enc (%) is the percentage of body weight used for encumbrance (Section 5.4). Prod is production time in days; Strength is the armor's durability for breakage. Maneuver / Ranged / Perception are activity penalties (see Section 7.2). For piecemeal armor and called shots, head and limb armor is treated as the torso AT if the appropriate type (Light = AT 2-3, Medium = 4-6, Heavy = 7-10); otherwise use the AT in parentheses (AT 3, 5, or 9).

name: Shields
notes: Table 6-4 (shield portion). A shield grants the listed DB against attacks it can block. Max Attacks Blocked is the maximum number of attacks the shield can stop per round. Cost, Prod (days), Weight, and Strength follow the same conventions as the Armor table.

name: Weapons
notes: Table 6-5. Cost in standard coinage; Prod Time to craft; Strength is the weapon's durability for breakage; Weight in pounds; Fumble is the upper end of the weapon's fumble range. Range lists the maximum distance for each band in order — Point-Blank (+20) / Short (0) / Medium (-10) / Long (-25) / Extreme (-50) / Absurd (-100); a leading — means the weapon has no Point-Blank band, and a blank Range means melee-only. Size Adj modifies the attack size. Attack Table is the combat table used to resolve the attack. * (halberd/poleaxe): the attacker may choose either attack table according to how the weapon is used. ** (hand crossbow): may be fired in one hand but requires two hands to load and ready. (2H) = two-handed.

name: Armor Types
notes: Table 7-2b. Describes each Armor Type (AT) used by the combat tables, with typical man-made and natural sources. The name's parenthetical (e.g. Flexible Cloth) is the generic flexibility/material classification.
rows: Those without armor have great incentive to not be hit at all, since there is nothing protecting them from the weapon.
Heavy clothes, hides, and furs are mostly used to protect against the elements although they do well at protecting against light slashes and provide some padding against crushing attacks.
Heavy clothing, furs, armor under-padding (haketon, arming coat), padded armor
Light hides or animals with significant fur

Flexible like clothing, the soft leather Armor Type is a heavier and thicker version of hides and furs and frequently used as the under-padding for heavier armors. It provides similar, but better protection, than heavy clothing.
Thick leather clothing, heavy armor under-padding, quilted armor (gambeson)
Heavy hide and/or thick heavy fur over hide, thin blubber, soft carapace

Hide Scale armors consist of small hard horn-like elements sewn together or to a flexible backing. Scaled Hide provides moderate protection against slashing attacks, although piercing weapons can more easily penetrate in between elements and joints.
Improved but similar protection to AT 4, Laminar consists of larger than hand sized horn-like elements connected together or overlapping in flexible joints.
Armadillo, overlapping rings of giant worm
The best protection organic armor can provide, AT 6 is made up of large rigid plates and provides good protection vs. all types of attacks. It is light but not as strong as metal.
Armored hides like rhinos, shell of a crab or armadillo, very thick blubber
Metal Scale is made up of small metal elements linked together, usually on a flexible backing. It provides very strong protection vs. piercing, strong vs. slashing, and less vs. crushing.
Lorica Squamata, Ringmail, Japanese kikko, jazerant, Chinese mountain pattern armor
Enchanted creatures with scaled hide as hard as metal

Mail is flexible and more maneuverable than other metal armors and provides excellent protection vs. slashing attacks, although piercing attacks can more easily penetrate it. It provides marginal protection vs. crushing attacks.
Maille, Viking mail-shirt (byrnie), Roman lorica hamata, Norman hauberk
Enchanted creatures with metallic or metal-hard fur

Brigandine armor, often called splinted, is made up of larger than hand sized metal plates. It provides most of the benefits of plate, without solid back and breastplate, therefore weaker vs. crushing and piercing attacks but much easier to wear.
Roman lorica segmentata, metal samurai armors
Enchanted creatures with overlapping bands, large scales or rings as hard as metal

Plate consists of larger metal plates centered on either a single torso barrel, or a pair of back and breast plates attached at the sides. Usually overlapping at the joints, it provides the best protection vs. all physical attacks.
Enchanted creatures with shells as hard as metal

name: Action Points
notes: Table 8-1. A round is 4 Action Points (AP). A cost shown as a range (e.g. Melee 2-4) lets the actor choose how many AP to spend; spending fewer than the maximum applies a penalty. 'C' marks a continuous action that occupies the remainder of the round. The first 0 AP (instantaneous) action each round is free; further instantaneous actions cost 1 AP each.

name: Movement
notes: Table 5-3. BMR = Base Movement Rate. Per Round / Per Phase give the distance moved at each pace (a phase = 1 AP; 4 phases per round). Penalty/AP is the pace penalty to actions taken while moving, or the AP cost to move that pace as a dedicated action. Load Limit is the maximum encumbrance at which the pace is possible. Dash requires 4 AP plus your instantaneous action. Backwards movement is half speed, maximum of Jog pace.

name: Charging
notes: Table 8-2. Charging into melee grants a Size bonus to the attack based on the speed of the approach.

name: Sizes
notes: Table 9-4a. Hit Multiplier scales a creature's hits by size. Hits Delivered (marked *) is an optional rule multiplying the hits a creature's attacks deliver. Larger attackers inflict more severe criticals against smaller foes (Table 9-4b); each size category by which a defender is smaller than the attack also grants the defender +5 DB (Table 9-5). Beyond size X (Enormous), the +1 row gives the per-size increment: +x1 Hit Multiplier and +x1 Hits Delivered for every x4 weight.

name: Size Adjustments to Attacks
notes: Table 9-4b. Adjust the severity of a critical by both the attack's size and the defender's size: a larger attack raises severity, a larger defender lowers it. Sum the two adjustments. Beyond Enormous, continue at +1 per size (attacker) / -1 per size (defender).

name: Attack Roll (Offensive / OB)
notes: Table 9-5 (offensive side). Modifiers to the attacker's Offensive Bonus (OB). * Flatfooted: the foe grants Surprise (+25) and, if in melee, a Rear attack (+35). Spending fewer than the maximum Action Points on the attack also applies the usual -25 per AP short.

name: Attack Roll (Defensive / DB)
notes: Table 9-5 (defensive side). Factors contributing to the defender's Defensive Bonus (DB). Dodge values are halved vs. Ranged attacks. Cover is x2 for hard cover; pole arms get ½ normal cover mods. ‡ Only one type of block and one type of dodge may be used; Passive Dodge and Passive Block are not cumulative. Passive Block + Shield DB may not exceed +50; Passive Dodge may not exceed +50.

name: Shields
notes: Table 9-6. A shield grants the listed DB against attacks it can block; Max Attacks is the number of attacks it can block per round. See Equipment > Shields for cost, weight, and strength.

name: Disarm Maneuvers
notes: Table 9-7a. A disarm is resolved as a conflicting maneuver — the disarmer rolls as a normal attack (including any parry penalty) plus the modifiers below; the defender makes a defense roll. Modifiers: Unarmed/grapple/crush attack -50; Blade attack -75; Piercing attack -100; Ranged attack -100*; Disarming a 2-H weapon -25*. (* cumulative with other penalties.) UM 66 = an Unusual Event on an unmodified roll of 66.
rows: Your attempt to disarm your foe is laughable. Not only do you fail to come close to disarming him, but you immediately fumble your weapon.
Sigh. Some days it just seems like the world is out to get you. You miss your opportunity. You fail to disarm. You fail to attack. You just fail. You're a failure.

Weird… it seems your foe is trying to disarm you, perhaps inadvertently, at the same time. If you fail this maneuver, your foe has disarmed you. If you succeed, you still manage to disarm your foe, but drop your weapon as well.
You can't partially disarm someone! Instead treat the disarm as a normal attack using this roll, but using all normal modifiers (and parry).
You successfully disarm your foe! The weapon falls to the ground (and must make a breakage check), but is within melee range.

You make disarming look easy as your foe gawks at you as you expertly remove him from his weapon. You may choose between taking the weapon for yourself or throw it up to 10' in a direction of your choice.

name: Katas
notes: Table 9-7b. A kata lets a combatant make multiple attacks in a single action at the penalty shown for the number of attacks (each additional foe adds -20). The listed bonuses apply for a light primary weapon and depending on the off-hand weapon.
rows: Off-hand weapon: dagger or any natural weapon
Off-hand weapon: any other light weapon

name: Protect
notes: Table 9-7c. A combatant may protect an ally by parrying or using a shield for them, taking the OB penalty below based on relative position (must be within melee range of both combatants). The penalty can be offset by the Battle Expertise skill.

name: Slaying
notes: Table 9-7d. A Slaying weapon or effect adds a direct bonus to the critical roll against the creatures it is designated to slay, based on its Slaying Type (I–V). Example: a broadsword +5 of orc Slaying II gives +10 to any critical rolls against orcs.

name: Subdual
notes: Table 9-7e. A subdual attack seeks to incapacitate rather than kill, taking the penalty below based on the attack type used. (* cumulative with other penalties.)

name: High Criticals
notes: Table 9-8a. Critical severities above E (the highest on the standard critical tables) are resolved as multiple criticals: roll an E plus the additional criticals indicated.

name: Hit Loss Penalties
notes: Table 9-8b. A penalty based on the percentage of total hits lost, applied to attacks, maneuvers, spellcasting, and full/partial blocking or dodging. These hit-loss penalties stack with other injury penalties.

name: Injuries & Recovery
notes:
Table 13-1a. The grid gives base recovery time (in days) by Recovery Roll result and injury type. Multipliers: Medium injuries x5, Head injuries x2, Severe injuries x10.

Recovery Roll = d100 (open-ended) + Body Development ranks + Co bonus + Healing Type modifier − Injury Penalty.

Injury severity (by penalty / bleeding): -1 to -20 = Light (1-3/rd); -21 to -40 = Medium (4-6/rd); < -40 = Severe (7+/rd).

Healing Type modifier: None -100; Medicine Success 0; Medicine Absolute Success +25; Magic or Herbalism Success +50; Magic and Herbalism Success +75.

name: Permanent Injury
notes: Table 13-1b. For Medium and Severe injuries, the Recovery roll determines whether a permanent injury develops; the residual permanent penalty is the listed percentage of the original injury penalty.

name: Cauterization
notes: Table 13-1c. Cauterizing a wound is a Routine (+30) maneuver (-5 per point of bleeding) requiring a torch or hot metal object. If a resulting Heat critical specifies an area other than the injured area, use the next lower result affecting the correct area. UM 66 = an Unusual Event on an unmodified roll of 66.
rows: Whose idea was it exactly to start waving around a flame? The fire has no effect on bleeding and the 'patient' takes a D Heat critical + 4d10 hits.
It was a noble attempt. You may have actually prevented more damage than you just caused. Bleeding is halved but patient takes a C Heat critical + 3d10 hits.

Hilarity ensues…depending on your viewpoint. The cauterization attempt is accompanied by mixed emotions from all involved, yet elicits giggles or guffaws from most. The patient ends up on fire, with actual results based on the final roll (Success or Failure).
Not a bad job really, you quite possibly saved a life. But was the cure worse? The bleeding stops but the patient takes a B Heat critical + 2d10 hits.
Not bad. You expertly apply the flame and stop the bleeding with only a small amount of screaming. Patient takes an A Heat critical + 1d10 hits.

From this point on you shall be referred to as the Flamemaster. The bleeding is stopped with no other damage. You are advised to not try this again so you can keep your reputation, and the Flamemaster name.

name: Defibrillation
notes: Table 13-2a. Used to restart a stopped heart; on success the heart restarts but the patient suffers an Electricity critical plus extra hits. UM 66 = an Unusual Event on an unmodified roll of 66. (The Failure row's hit dice print as '- 3d10' in the source, but follow the table's pattern of additional hits — +3d10.)
rows: You reflect on the decisions that brought you here. They weren't good. There is no effect on the patient's heart but he takes a D Electricity critical + 4d10 hits.
You weren't completely sure this was going to work, so it's no real surprise that it doesn't. Patient takes a C Electricity critical + 3d10 hits.

The electricity coursing through the patient has a deleterious effect. The patient babbles incoherently as he has a near-death experience either before he dies (failed maneuver), or before he lives again (successful maneuver)! Hopefully he'll live to tell the tale.
A bit messy and certainly not harmless, but effective. Patient's heart restarts, but he takes a B Electricity critical + 2d10 hits.
Far better than you expected, and certainly the best one could hope for. Patient's heart restarts, but he takes an A Electricity critical + 1d10 hits.

Bards will sing songs of this far and wide. You will be famous and live long in folks’ hearts. Unless, of course, your patient is a nobody, in which case no one will sing and no one will remember. No one cares.

name: Decomposition
notes: Table 13-2b. As a body decomposes after death it passes through these stages; the Effect is the penalty applied to attempts to revive the body.

name: Reactions
notes: Table 14-1. Roll d100 (open-ended) + a situation modifier (typically ±10, rarely beyond ±30) + applicable Social skill ranks if a good first impression was made, then consult the table for the NPC's initial reaction. UM 66 = an Unusual Event on an unmodified roll of 66.
rows: NPC immediately dislikes character and will leave or ask/force character to leave. Make a morale check for the NPC, who will attack (success) or withdraw (failure).
NPC will interact with the character, but with a noticeable reserve. NPC will keep an eye on the character and be ready for combat.

NPC mistakes the character for someone completely different that he dislikes (hostile or wary result) or someone he likes (receptive or friendly result). A neutral result means a mistaken identification without a positive or negative opinion associated with it.
NPC has no significant initial reaction to the character, and will interact without modifiers. NPC will be alert for trouble, but will interact normally.
NPC likes the character’s initial approach and will be open to interaction. NPC will relax, but will not completely let his guard down.

NPC is happy to see the character, and will go out of his way to engage with him. NPC will relax as much as is reasonable in the situation, and will initiate interaction if the character does not do so first.

name: Fright Level
notes: Table 14-3a. The Fright Level rows give the level/difficulty of the fear-provoking situation (the +3/+5 rows are cumulative modifiers for supernatural elements); the character makes a Fear resistance roll against it. The Failure Result rows give the outcome by how much the roll is failed, including the modifier to subsequent Rally rolls (Table 14-3c).
rows: You are confronted with immediate danger.
An armed opponent is actively trying to harm you.
The fight is going badly, you are outnumbered, half of your allies are down or fled, or group is the target of a lethal ambush.
You are outnumbered and becoming surrounded, a superior enemy force is beginning to overrun your position, or more than half your allies are down or fled.
You face a dangerous foe you cannot harm, or more than three quarters of your allies are down or fled.
You are confronted with an unstoppable threat, or more than 90% of your allies are down or fled.
The threat includes powerful or unfamiliar supernatural or magical elements.
The threat includes powerful and unfamiliar supernatural or magical elements.

Character will not advance or pursue the enemy, but will fight if the enemy comes to him. Outside of a combat situation, the character will remove themself from the situation as quickly as they are able, possibly making excuses for a hasty departure.
Character will try to escape the situation, but in a controlled manner, not turning his back on the danger. If the character cannot flee, and the penalty for surrender is mild, he will surrender.

Character will flee using at least running pace until collapsing from fatigue or reaching a place of safety where the character can no longer perceive the source of fear. Routed combatants will attack anyone who attempts to physically block retreat. If they cannot flee, they will surrender unless they are certain surrender will result in death.

Character will flee at top speed until collapsing from fatigue. Such characters will drop weapons and gear if it allows them to flee faster. They will ignore attempts to physically stop them if at all possible. Broken combatants who cannot flee will surrender regardless of possible consequences. (The characters will drop their weapons and fall to the floor in abject terror.)

name: Morale
notes: Table 14-3b. A morale check tests whether a follower carries out an order, resolved with the leader's Leadership skill. UM 66 = an Unusual Event on an unmodified roll of 66.
rows: Your follower sullenly refuses or even reacts with hostility. You offended him somehow and get -10 to Morale until you figure out what it is.
Follower refuses. You're not sure if he is being purposefully difficult or even why. Sometimes you are just better off doing things yourself.

Somehow, your follower completely misunderstands what is being asked and doesn't ask for clarification, so instead does something completely different. However, this either works out (Success or higher) in a weird way, or doesn't (Failure or less).
There is some confusion on what is desired and your follower requires further explanation. Where do you get these guys, dial-a-minion?
You expertly convey your desires to your follower who follows them to the best of his ability. Hopefully he actually has some ability.
You are destined for an executive position. Follower not only does as is ordered, but is inspired by his new hero and role-model. Follower's fan-boy status grants +5 to Morale rolls for 1 month.

name: Rally
notes: Table 14-3c. A leader who can be seen and heard may rally followers who failed a fear check; the roll modifier comes from Table 14-3a (use the worst-affected follower when rallying a group, with an extra -10 per 10 followers). Success reduces the fear severity; failure can worsen it. UM 66 = an Unusual Event on an unmodified roll of 66.
rows: You manage to say all the wrong things, at the wrong times, which only ends up scaring your followers even more. Increase fear by 1 severity.
You are quite ineffective at rallying, or perhaps it's just the chaos around you is distracting everyone. Next time just speak louder.

In trying to rally your followers (which may or may not work), you manage to get yourself all fired up. With a rush of adrenaline and positive energy you feel invincible, and scared of nothing! You get +20 to Fear RRs today, and +5 hits.
Your message comes out as a jumble of words, but manages to have a positive effect on your followers. Reduce fear by 1 severity.
Well spoken Commander! Your soothing words calm your men, who are proud to be following your lead. Reduce fear by 2 severities.

You give an awe-inspiring speech that makes even foes consider (however briefly) joining your worthy cause. Men scared to tears are once again ready to take up arms beside you. Reduce fear by 3 severities.

name: Biomes
notes: Table 15-1. Biome types grouped by region, with their single-letter map code, typical precipitation, and example flora and fauna.
rows: Varies by elevation; as forests (low), shrublands (mid), or tundra (high)
Varies; mountain goats, alpacas, marmots, elk, beetles
Shellfish, fish, some mammals, crustaceans, turtles
Many insects of all sizes, burrowers, reptiles, amphibians, bats
Virtually none, mosses and lichens only in extremely sheltered areas
Seals, penguins, walruses, arctic foxes, arctic terns, whales, polar bears
Low shrubs, few dwarf trees, sedges, mosses, lichen, grasses, flowers
Mosquitoes, flies, bees, caribou, seals, wolves, migratory birds
Evergreen conifers: pine, fir, spruce, taiga, and blue spires. Some oak, maple, elm, poplar in warmer regions.
Chipmunks, weasels, foxes, wolves, deer, bear, large herbivores (moose); owls, hawks
Evergreen or deciduous shrubs with small leaves or spines, succulents
Burrowing animals, reptiles such as lizards and snakes, owls
Perennial grasses and flowering plants adapted to drought, fire, and cold
Grazing animals, wild horses, canines, rodents, weasels, badgers, foxes
Broadleaf and coniferous trees – oak, hickory, maple, cedar; flowering herbs
Most boreal fauna; squirrels, rabbits. Reptiles & amphibians, snakes, turtles, mountain lions, timber wolf
Ground-hugging shrubs, short woody trees, cacti
Burrowers, kangaroo rats, insects, arachnids, reptiles, and birds
Perennial grasses 3'+ tall. Drought and fire resistant trees and shrubs
Hoofed mammals, antelopes, cats, canines, elephants, giraffes, rhinos
Large variety of trees, orchids, vines, ferns, mosses, and palms
Simians, large cats, variety of insects, birds

name: Extreme Temperatures
notes: Table 15-3. For each round spent in an extreme environment (below about -50°F / -49°C or above about 200°F / 100°C) without appropriate protection, roll the indicated Cold or Heat critical. Beyond the listed ranges, each additional +500°F / +300°C adds +1 critical severity.

name: Poisons
notes: Table 15-6a. Poison effects by type and severity. On exposure the target makes a Resistance Roll; the severity suffered depends on how much the RR is failed by. Start is the delay before effects begin and Duration is how long they last (rds = rounds). Effects are cumulative — a more severe effect also includes the lesser effects of the same type.
rows: swelling around the point of injection or ingestion; drowsiness (-15); slightly blurred vision (-25)
lessening of motor coordination in legs (-25 for leg maneuvers) and arms (-25 for hand maneuvers); mild euphoria (-20 to all actions)
discoloration and chills; incapacitating headaches (no activity; 1-10 hits/round until unconsciousness or coma)
death due to circulatory failure (i.e., oxygen starvation and associated waste poisoning)
queasiness, cramps and an upset stomach (-20 to all actions)
painful cramps and vomiting (-50 all actions, 10% chance of incapacity each round)

partial conversion (1-100%) of bodily tissue to another form (with associated disability; death if area affected is a critical organ); victim at -51 to -100; lapse into unconsciousness after 1-50 rounds
complete transformation of bodily tissue to another form; death if area is critical to survival
lightheadedness and swelling (-10); waves of pain (-10 to -50 1d5x10 in any given round)
moderate loss of overall coordination (-30); in any given round, there is 5% chance the muscles necessary to a given action will not operate
fever and sweating; lapses into unconsciousness
death due to overall muscle failure (e.g., cessation of heart activity)
mild loss of thought and motor coordination (-30)
nervous system shock (-50 to all actions)
permanent nerve and brain damage (-10 to -100 to all actions)
general mild numbness and lack of feeling, slightly slurred speech (-20)
total numbness, and difficulty moving, speech seriously affected (-50)
death due to cardiac and respiratory failure
great pain (-10 to -50 in any given round, 1d5x10)
wracking pain and sweating blood (-20 to -100 in any given round, 2d5x10)
incapacitation; after 1-100 minutes lapse into coma
death due to dissolution of vital tissue, destruction of associated organs (e.g., liver reduced to fluid)
significant euphoria (-50); painful choking and coughing (-10 to -50 any given round, 1d5x10)
uncontrollable coughing (1-10 hits/round for 1-10 rounds); lapse into unconsciousness for 1d10 hours; 50% chance of coma for 1-10 days.
death due to respiratory failure and associated oxygen starvation

name: Diseases
notes: Table 15-6b. Disease effects by type and severity; the severity suffered depends on how much the victim's Resistance Roll is failed by. After the listed duration, recovery uses the Poison/Disease column of the recovery table (Section 13.1).
rows: Inflammations and swelling in the glands, particularly in the armpit and groin areas (-25). There is a 10% chance per limb or eye of immobilizing pus-filled infections. (Limb or eye cannot be used until cured.)
Lesions ooze pus; bleeding through pores; high fever; complete immobilization (50% chance of coma for 1-50 days)
Death due to viral or bacterial overload in 1-20 days.
No significant permanent damage; withdrawal results in mild spasms, sweating, and irritability (-15).

Occasional hallucinations (5% chance -25); intermittent spasms (5% chance -35); double vision (10% chance -40); impaired Memory and Agility (-1 to -3 to stat bonus). Withdrawal results in immobility (5% chance of death).
Frequent hallucinations (10% chance -50); intermittent fevers; shaking; all temporary stats reduced by 1d100 (minimum of 1). Withdrawal results in immobility (20% chance of death). Lasts 1d10 days.
A slow death (1-50 months) due to destruction of internal organs (e.g., liver, kidneys, intestines). Withdrawal results in immobility (40% chance of death).
Redness, swelling and pain make it harder to use the affected limb (-10 penalty). Intermittent fever makes thinking fuzzy (-10 to -30 penalty to all actions, 1d3x10 on any given round).

Severe swelling of wound area with eruptions of pus. Tenderness and swelling moves from wound toward heart along veins in red streaks. Pain in limb makes use hard (-25 penalty), and fever causes dizziness and difficulty thinking (-10 to -50 on any given round, 1d5x10).

Area around wound blackens and leaks foul fluids, blackened areas begin spreading toward heart along veins. Pain and numbness renders limb unusable (-100), and severe fever makes it difficult to think (-20 to -100 on any given round, 2d5x10). Lasts 1d10 days. Without magical or antibiotic cure, victim will slip to the extreme level at the end of this duration, unless the limb is amputated.
Gangrenous flesh is green, purple, and black. Toxins from rotting tissue spread through blood causing widespread organ failures and death in 2d10 hours.
Fever, sweating, coughing, dizziness and chest pain (-20)
Raging fever (-20 to -100 on any given round, 2d5x10)
Death due to viral or bacterial overload in 1-100 hours.
Mild fever, cramps, and diarrhea (-10 to all actions and double need for water)

Occasional outbreaks of fever (any time the host rolls an unmodified 11, -20 penalty for 24 hours), compromised immune system (-25 to poison and disease RRs). Effects last until disease is cured or host dies

Overload of parasites affects blood chemistry and brain tissue leading to irrational and sometimes hostile behavior (any time the host rolls an unmodified 22, lasting 24 hours). Delirium affects all actions (-20 to all actions). Affects last until disease is cured or host dies.
Body overwhelmed by parasites and their waste products, leading to death in 1d10 days.

name: Lighting & Visibility
notes: Table 15-7 (lighting). For Penalty and Nightvision, use the first number if sight is necessary for the action and the second if sight is merely helpful. Creatures with Nightvision reduce the penalty by the listed amount. Many objects casting shadows from a single light source can worsen the effective lighting.
rows: many bright light sources, outdoors in daylight
outdoors with tree cover, multiple lanterns
Utterdark spell, underground with no light source

name: Fog & Mist
notes: Table 15-7 (fog & mist). Visibility is the range sight is possible within the fog; Perception is the penalty to Perception rolls. Auditory perception for sounds beyond visual range is at an additional -20; smoke/smog affects smell perception by -10 to -50 depending on strength.

name: Skill Similarity
notes: Table 3-0c. When using a skill in place of a related one, apply the Skill Relation penalty for how the two skills are related, then adjust for how Similar the situations are. Example: using Riding: Horse on a camel is the same skill, different specialization (-25) but very similar (+0), for a net -25.

name: Information Spell RRs
notes: Table 2-5. The target of an informational spell gets one Resistance Roll per casting; how well the RR is made determines the target's awareness. The 'point of origin' is revealed only if the spell is directed specifically at the target (not a general area) and the caster is visible to the target.
rows: Target is not aware of the spell; spell results work against this target.
Target has an “odd” feeling (prickling hairs on the neck, feeling of being watched, etc.), but cannot identify it specifically. Spell results still work against this target.

Target has the same odd feeling as the result above, but in this case can identify the source. (See text.) This does not let the target see the spell effect if it is not visible, nor does this reveal what the spell was, but the target definitely knows someone is casting an informational spell that has targeted him. Spell results still work against this target.

Target not only feels the spell effect and can identify where it is coming from, but also has a vague sense of what it was trying to discover. (“Someone just tried to probe my mind”, or “Someone is searching for something”). Spell fails against this target.

name: Spell-Casting Modifications
notes: Table 4-2. Modifiers to the Spellcasting roll; use the column matching the caster's realm (or hybrid). — = not applicable. Enc% = the caster's encumbrance percentage. Misc. modifiers (apply to all realms, not shown in the grid): Fast casting -25 per Action Point below the maximum; Overcasting -20 per spell level above the caster's level; Preparation +10/round (max +20); plus any general penalties (vary).

name: Resistance Roll Modifiers
notes: Table 4-5. Modifiers to a target's Resistance Roll against a spell. The armor bonuses are realm-specific; a target facing a Hybrid caster gets the bonus versus all of that caster's realms.
rows: Metal armor vs. Essence or Channeling

name: Magical Ritual Modifiers
notes: Table 5-1a. Modifiers to the casting roll for a magical ritual. Ranged values (e.g. +5 to +25) are set by the GM based on circumstances.
rows: Level of caster above level of spell
Level of caster below level of spell
Caster does not know the spell list from which the spell is cast
Caster knows the list, but not to the level of the spell cast
Caster knows list to the spell's level
Each rank in list caster has over the spell's level
Each additional spell included beyond the first
Spell is from caster's own Base list
Spell is Evil (and caster has learned some Evil list)
Spell is Evil (and caster has not learned an Evil list)
Spell is not from caster's own Realm
Ritual uses PP from a single Realm
Ritual uses PP from two Realms
Ritual uses PP from all three Realms

name: Investing Power
notes: Table 5-1b. A ritual's casting roll improves by investing additional Power Points beyond the spell's cost; the bonus follows a square progression — investing +n² PP grants +3n.

name: Investing Time
notes: Table 5-1c. Spending more time on a ritual improves its casting roll. 3 minutes is the minimum ritual casting time. Once casting time grows long enough, the primary caster and major contributors must make an Endurance roll of the listed difficulty as each time mark is passed.

name: Blood Investment
notes: Table 5-1d. A ritual's casting roll can be boosted by an investment of blood — concussion hits deliberately taken (careful bleeding or ritualistic damage), or a critical inflicted on a sacrifice. The hit progression doubles for each step (max 32d10).
rows: +20 per degree of crit severity (up to +100)

name: Ritual Items
notes: Table 5-1e. A ritual gains a bonus from valuable tools/fixtures used, or equivalently from a sacrifice or expended component of the listed value. * The bonus assumes generally appropriate items: -5 if less than ideal (but accepted by the GM), +5 for a broadly appropriate item, +10 for a specifically appropriate item. The pattern continues beyond the listed values.

name: Spell Parameters
notes: Table 5-2. Modifiers to a ritual's casting roll for extending or altering a spell's parameters. '(C)' denotes a concentration requirement.
rows: Each step crit is altered (e.g. A to B)
Increasing Area of Effect, for each doubling
Increase Spell range, for each doubling
For each step change of time duration

name: Magical Ritual Maneuver Resolution
notes: Table 5-3. Resolves the outcome of a magical ritual after all modifiers. UM 66 = an Unusual Event on an unmodified roll of 66.
rows: Catastrophic ritual failure: roll for spell failure, adding the PP in the ritual to the roll. Apply result to primary caster and major contributors.
Ritual fails. Roll for spell failure without modifiers and apply the results to primary caster and major contributors. All efforts wasted.

Whether the ritual succeeds or fails in its intended purpose, it disturbed the Essence, producing a side effect. This could be anything from turning every participants’ eyes orange to opening a portal to an alien world. Magnitude of side effect will generally be proportional to the power in the ritual.

Ritual succeeds, but roll spell failure anyway (no modifier). Ignore results about losing or not losing the PP or the spell effect, since the PP went into the effect, which worked. Apply any other effects, however.
Ritual succeeds, and its casting level is increased by 50%. Sweet.

name: Bard Songs
notes: Table 10-1. Resolution for casting Bard Songs. Duration is expressed in terms of the bard's ranks in the song's list; RR Mod. adjusts targets' Resistance Rolls. SCR = Spell Casting Roll. UM 66 = an Unusual Event on an unmodified roll of 66.
rows: Loss of confidence as well as the spell. No maneuver with this performance skill can succeed until you have slept. For the same period all Bard Base list spells will suffer a -10 modification to the SCR.

You inadvertently tap into the full power of your art. You cast an additional random Essence spell up to the level of the Song cast. The spell is cast at your level; if target selection is required, you know range, area of effect, and type (Elemental, Force, etc.) of the spell, but not the exact spell or what list it is from.

name: Black Channels
notes: Table 10-7. Example Black Channel attack spells by tier. P = permanent duration. Black Channels III effects are lethal — death occurs 1-10 rounds after the target is affected, with incapacitation until then. The RR modifier for Thrayn's Touch does not include the penalty for touch range.
rows: Disrupts body cells; foe operates at -50
Nerve disruption; foe is sent into coma
Severe brain disorder, convulsive spasms; foe operates at -75
Foe is in a coma; +10 hits
Erases facial area, leaves blank skin

name: Animal Maneuvers
notes: Table 3-1. Animal category maneuver (Absolute Maneuver), used by the Animal Handling and Riding skills. UM 66 = Unusual Event on an unmodified roll of 66.
rows: Your confidence breaks at a crucial moment and the animal seizes the moment to lash out (attacking at +30) before bolting in a random direction.
Perhaps it is your impatient manner, perhaps it is your over-abrupt movements, perhaps your smelly cologne, but the animal is unimpressed and uninfluenced.

The animal recognizes you not as its master but in some other role such as offspring, parent, or mate. Its attempts to protect and feed, follow and imitate, or woo and placate you may be helpful or problematic, depending on the success of your maneuver.
For a moment you think the animal is receptive to your guidance, but a sudden distraction breaks the moment. You may try again at +10 if appropriate.
Whispering calming words while projecting a sure and confident demeanor, you have the animal's attention and cooperation. Do not waste your opportunity.
Your insight into this animal's nature has won you its lasting respect. You will receive +30 to any future Animal maneuvers versus this target (until an Absolute Failure is achieved).

name: Awareness Maneuvers
notes: Table 3-2. Awareness category maneuver (Absolute Maneuver), used by the Perception and Tracking skills. UM 66 = Unusual Event on an unmodified roll of 66.
rows: You glean enormous amounts of information about your target… all of it wrong. You utterly misinterpret what you perceive and are sure that you are correct.
Attempting to emulate the perceptive abilities of an eggplant, you notice nothing useful. There is a great future waiting for you as an art critic.

Your surroundings trigger a flashback to your past and consumes your attention for 3 rounds while you relive the experience. You may immediately make a Lore maneuver to recall something previously missed but important to the story. Your maneuver resolves after.
Your interest is piqued, but you glean minimal information. You may try again in 6 rounds at a +10, if appropriate.
Following your instincts you focus your attention, allowing your finely-tuned perception to reveal that which you seek.
Elementary my dear boy! You identify with casual ease the target or information you seek, along with contextual information not obvious to a lesser eye.

name: Adrenal Maneuvers
notes: Table 3-4. Adrenal maneuver (Absolute Maneuver; requires 1 round of concentration), used by the Body Discipline skills (Adrenal Defense, Adrenal Focus, Adrenal Speed, Adrenal Strength) and by Fortitude (Brawn). Benefits last 1 round per full 20 the maneuver succeeds by. UM 66 = Unusual Event on an unmodified roll of 66.
rows: What were you thinking? Your feeble attempt at focus creates a mental block that hinders your future attempts, giving you a -25 to this skill (until an Absolute Success is achieved).
You fail to summon the inner focus necessary for this feat, and receive no benefit or effects from your attempt. Thanks for playing.

As you begin to summon your focus, you notice an unfamiliar darkness within yourself. You enter a cleansing trance that lasts the entire round. If you succeed at your maneuver treat as an Absolute Success. If you fail, treat as an Absolute Failure.
You are distracted at a critical moment, but receive half the normal benefit. Or, if not in combat, you may abort and try again with a +10. Try switching to decaf.
You are a paragon of self-control and receive full benefits of the maneuver for 1 round for every 20 you succeeded by (1 round for 101-120, 2 for 121-140, etc.), doubled for Adrenal Defense.
Wow! You not only succeed, but you have tapped into a reservoir of strength within yourself. As Success, plus you receive double benefits for the first round.

name: Composition Maneuvers
notes: Table 3-8. Composition category maneuver (Absolute Maneuver), used by the Illusion Crafting, Musical Composition, and Writing skills. UM 66 = Unusual Event on an unmodified roll of 66.
rows: You artist types! In a fit of anger you destroy any work you have already accomplished. Your emotions get the better of you and you are -25 to this skill (until an Absolute Success is achieved).
Whatever is locked in your head remains so. You find yourself utterly unable to transfer your concept to the medium. Perhaps another day will yield improvements.

Your interpretation is a profound departure from your normal style, from any style in fact. People will be unable to understand it and only leave confused. In a few days of contemplation they will see it as either the brilliant (success) or bad (failure) piece that it was.
Your temper flares as your attempts fall short of your expectations. While your work has merit, you have not effectively grasped your own intent.
Twas nothing! You have once again demonstrated your ample genius to all with the ability to see. If only the whole world were not blind compared to you.
Ahh, Bach! The masterstroke of your piece falls into place with the surety of perfection. This piece will make your reputation, if not your fortune. Who says you have to die to be great?

name: Crafting Maneuvers
notes: Table 3-9. Crafting category maneuver (Absolute Maneuver), used by the Culinary, Drawing/Painting, Fabric Craft, Leathercraft, Metalcraft, Stonecraft, and Woodcraft skills. UM 66 = Unusual Event on an unmodified roll of 66.
rows: You fail miserably but are so oblivious you are convinced you have succeeded admirably. Your new misguided approach gives you -25 to this skill (until an Absolute Success is achieved).
Your skills have deserted you. You waste your effort, and create extra work for yourself. You may attempt to salvage the materials with a crafting maneuver with an additional penalty of -25.

You have a great idea for a unique style for your craftwork, and after an arduous effort you realize the work. The result has a unique built-in feature that either hinders the work (if a Failure), or makes it more desirable or useful (if Success).
You know what you want to accomplish, but it just keeps falling short. Your work is functional, but deficient with increased chance of breakage (-10 Strength).
Your hands are sure, and your work is precise and artful. Your work comes out exactly as you intended and there are no surprises along the way.
Your diligence is rewarded with your latest masterwork! Everyone can see the remarkable quality of your work, and your abilities will be more highly regarded than ever. Item has +10 Strength.

name: Attunement Maneuvers
notes: Table 3-10a. Delving maneuver (Absolute Maneuver) for the Attunement skill. UM 66 = Unusual Event on an unmodified roll of 66.
rows: You not only fail to attune, you also manage to deattune yourself to any items you have already attuned to. You may attempt to reattune to those items at a -20.
You fail to match auras with the item, and a period of contemplation is in order. You may not try again until you have developed another rank of Attunement.

You glimpse a series of visions associated with the item's past over 3 rounds during which time you are in an unwakeable trance. When you emerge from the trance you will know about the item's past, even if you've not successfully attuned to it.
You learn one ability of the item, but you cannot use it. You may try again to attune to the item after a good night's sleep.
You learn and may use one ability of the item. You may make a maneuver at +20 to try to gain more knowledge. If you fail you cannot try again for 24 hours.
You harmonize your aura with that of the item smoothly, learning all abilities of the item, which are now available for your use. If only success looked as impressive as you feel about yourself.

subtables.title: Modifiers
rows: Does not know realm of item
Attuning a known property or spell of item

name: Rune Maneuvers
notes: Table 3-10b. Delving maneuver (Absolute Maneuver) for the Runes skill (deciphering a stored rune spell). UM 66 = Unusual Event on an unmodified roll of 66.
rows: You accidentally cast the Rune (targetting yourself) upon trying to decipher it. Learn to read without mumbling to yourself.
It is all gibberish to you. Until you improve your understanding (develop another rank in Runes), you may not try again.

Yes! Those marks are more than a spell, they contain a secret message! Resolve the maneuver as normal. It then takes you 1-10 hours of study (taken at the first opportunity) to conclude that you were mistaken…or were you? (psst, GM…insert plot hook here).
You learn the identity of the spell inscribed in the Rune, but cannot use it. You may try again after a good night's sleep, but another Partial Success is treated as Failure.
Your mystical senses are at your beck. You understand the Rune, identify the spell, and may cast it. Life is good when things turn out like this.

You are a master of the written arts. As Success, but you also notice any unusual features of the Rune (or the material it is inscribed on), if any. You will become legendary, as songs are sung of your amazing abilities!

subtables.title: Modifiers
rows: Does not know realm of Rune
Knows realm and spell of rune

name: Piloting Maneuvers
notes: Table 3-11. Modifiers to the Piloting skill maneuver (operating a vehicle), applied to a standard maneuver roll based on the vehicle's capacity and maneuverability, the pace driven, and the sharpness of any turn.

name: Gymnastic Maneuvers
notes: Table 3-12. Gymnastic category maneuver (Absolute Maneuver), used by the Acrobatics, Contortions, and Jumping skills. UM 66 = Unusual Event on an unmodified roll of 66.
rows: In your zeal, you have forgotten to warm up and severely pull a major muscle (-25) and are stunned for two rounds. Good job buddy!
You freeze at a critical moment, failing your maneuver. If appropriate, you may make an additional roll to abort your maneuver. Otherwise, take your chances.

Regardless of your success or failure, you manage to make yourself look completely foolish. If no one was watching, you're lucky. Otherwise you will never hear the end of the jokes and stories about it. Surely there's some way to make up for it, or make everyone forget...
Everything was going so well, but you cannot seem to follow through, and it takes longer. If appropriate make an additional maneuver at +10. Otherwise you fail.
Fancy footwork, my friend! You complete the maneuver completely as expected and in fine form, and barely break a sweat. Must be the shoes.

You skip a light fandango, turn cartwheels 'cross the floor and complete the maneuver with the kind of precision and grace you have always dreamed about. Onlookers gawk as you make the impossible look easy.

name: Knowledge Tiers
notes: Table 3-13a. A rough guide to a character's level of knowledge in a Lore (academic) skill based on the number of ranks held.
rows: Introductory knowledge with details on a few topics.
Good understanding and recall of all major topic areas, as well as basic details.
Mastery and recall of all important aspects of topic, plus many finer details in most areas.
Ability to form multiple complex inferences, create accurate analogies, and form connections to other subject matters.
Complete understanding and knowledge of obscure and uncommon parts of subject matter.

name: Lore Maneuvers
notes: Table 3-13b. Lore category maneuver (Absolute Maneuver), used by all Lore skills (Creature Lore, Historic Lore, Materials Lore, Racial Lore, Region Lore, Religion/Philosophy, Spell Lore) and the Language skill. May instead be resolved as a Percentage Maneuver to gauge how much is recalled. UM 66 = Unusual Event on an unmodified roll of 66.
rows: What's all this? You irretrievably confuse yourself and cannot possibly recall this information without at least 12 hours to refamiliarize yourself with the topic.
Hmmm. Nope. Nothing springs to mind. Perhaps if you were to spend another six hours studying the matter…

Great Horny Toads! You've been using that word for years and suddenly realized you were pronouncing it completely wrong! You whoop and gurgle happily, rolling your mouth around the new sound…why is everyone staring at you?
You recall some details of the subject, but nothing specific. Stew on this for an hour or so and something might come to you. (Roll again in 1 hour).
Ah, of course! A simple fact, of course, right on the tip of your tongue. You recall all relevant details about the subject.
With a nonchalant air, you relate the details of the topic from memory, including relevant quotations and analysis by authorities of note. Now who's the savant around here?

name: Language Tiers
notes: Table 3-13c. Spoken and written fluency conferred by the number of ranks in a Language skill. Most languages are considered proficient at 4-6 ranks.
rows: Recognition of the language and communication of very simple concepts that can be expressed with a single word (e.g., eat, danger, money, enemy, pain, etc.).
Recognition of the language in written form and the meaning of a few simple words and phrases.
Communication of moderately basic concepts in the form of phrases at a slow rate. Understanding the tone/context of speech spoken at a normal rate, but no more.
User can get an overview of simple writings, but will have trouble with details. User may write very short sentences of a moderately simple nature (i.e., at third grade level).
Conversation on simple subjects using whole sentences instead of broken phrases. User can understand everyday conversation when spoken slowly. Recognition of major dialects.
User can read moderately simple writings and understand most of the details, but still has trouble with subtle concepts. User may write sentences at a fifth grade level.

User may converse in everyday conversation of an average nature (e.g. market talk, peasant discussion, conversation with guards) but rapid or sophisticated speech is still troublesome, and the user has a notable accent.
Reading of most everyday writings and normal books, and writing at a seventh grade level although the user's writing will be recognized as uneducated or foreign.
Normal speaking level of the native population, the common man. Subtle or sophisticated concepts still prove troublesome, and the user cannot understand dialects or archaic speech out of the norm.
Reading level of a reasonably literate person, and writing at a ninth grade level.

True fluency, allowing understanding of, and conversation with, the most learned of native speakers. Sophisticated folk may still recognize you as an outsider, and archaic or unusual concepts may still prove troublesome.
Reading level of an educated member of the nobility and writing at an eleventh grade level.
Fluency plus the ability to recognize the regional and cultural origin of speech (though difficult dialects will still prove troublesome).
College writing level of an average nature. Allows one to read relatively complex material and recognize (but not fully understand) the nature of archaic or unusual dialects and concepts.
Absolute fluency in chosen dialect plus simple understanding and speaking ability in related dialects.
Writing complex or highly technical passages in the chosen dialect, as well as the ability to work at a moderate level in related dialects.
Absolute fluency of the chosen language and all closely related dialects. Only the most archaic and complex concepts may prove troublesome.
Ability to read and write in a variety of dialects and even in archaic forms of the language. Only the most obscure concepts and forms will prove troublesome.

name: Medical Maneuvers
notes: Table 3-15. Medical category maneuver, used by the Herbalism, Medicine, and Poison Mastery skills. UM 66 = Unusual Event on an unmodified roll of 66.
rows: The herb is not only ruined, it has become slightly toxic. If treating a patient, you manage to make things worse, increasing any injury penalty by -10.
The herb or poison and your pride are ruined. If treating a patient, the Recovery Roll is made at -100 (as if they received no care).

Your preparation of the herb or poison has altered its properties in a way that may be useful (on a Success) or not (on a Failure). If treating a patient, in addition to success or failure treating the condition, you've improved or worsened a different condition by +/-10.
The herb or poison is half as potent. If treating a patient, your misguided care provides minimal improvement. The Recovery Roll is made at a modifier of -25.
The herb or poison is ready! If treating a patient, you are alert enough to catch problems as they arise and they should recover normally (+0 to the Recovery Roll).
The herb or poison has doubled potency. If treating a patient, you know exactly what to do and they respond miraculously to your care (add +25 to the Recovery Roll).

name: Mental Discipline Maneuvers
notes: Table 3-16. Mental Discipline category maneuver, used by the Control Lycanthropy, Meditation, and Mental Focus skills. UM 66 = Unusual Event on an unmodified roll of 66.
rows: You are assailed by doubts and distractions. Your feeble attempt at focus creates a mental block that hinders your future attempts, giving you a -25 to this skill (until an Absolute Success is achieved).
You call forth your discipline and… what was that noise? Sorry, trying to focus here. Why won't that itch go away? Your effort is insufficient to get past the distractions.

Carefully you follow the path of a thought before realizing it is seemingly unrelated to your task. If you succeed at your maneuver, the unexpected insight is treated as an Absolute Success. If you fail, it has led you far astray to an Absolute Failure.
Your discipline is assailed by distractions and you find it difficult to focus. You receive half the usual benefit, or may try again next round with a +10 modification.
Summoning all your training, you concentrate on your breathing and empty your mind, permitting yourself to focus completely on the task at hand.
You slip easily into a state of perfect focus, easily putting aside all distractions and doubts. You receive x2 normal benefits and a +10 on subsequent rolls to maintain this state.

name: Performance Art Maneuvers
notes: Table 3-18. Performance Art category maneuver (Absolute Maneuver), used by the Acting, Music, and Stage Magic skills. UM 66 = Unusual Event on an unmodified roll of 66.
rows: Horrors! In what is most certainly the worst performance of your career, you manage to offend everyone in your audience, giving you -25 to this skill (until an Absolute Success is achieved).
A lackluster performance leaves your audience yawning. The snores drown out the feeble applause. Maybe you should join the circus.

Unbeknownst to you, two rival factions were in the audience and you have given offense to one of them. You find yourself embroiled in their raging dispute overshadowing how your performance actually was. Some days it just doesn't pay to get out of bed.
Not bad. Not good, but not bad. You hold the attention of the audience, but a few key blunders spoil an otherwise satisfactory performance.
Bravo! Your performance is a success, and the enthusiastic response of your audience warms your old performer's heart.
You have surpassed yourself, and your audience mobs you for autographs, bits of string from your outfit, and kisses. What will you do for an encore? It is best to not keep your audience waiting.

name: Channeling Modifiers
notes: Table 3-19a. Modifiers to the Channeling skill maneuver (Power Manipulation), used to transmit Power Points to a willing recipient or a storage item. Resolved as a Percentage Maneuver; the result is the percentage of PP successfully transmitted.

name: Power Projection Maneuvers
notes: Table 3-19b. Power Manipulation maneuver for the Power Projection skill (a power-projection duel between casters). UM 66 = Unusual Event on an unmodified roll of 66.
rows: Your reach has exceeded your grasp. Treat as a Failure and additionally roll on the Spell Failure Table “Force” column and add the number of hits taken.
You have lost this projection, which bruises your body as well as your ego. Take Hits equal to both sides' PP total in the projection.

Your projection inadvertently creates a magical vacuum, which flares up briefly and sucks additional power from both participants. Each participant must immediately expend another 1 PP, which is added to the projection pool.
You almost lose your hold on the projection, but may recover if you immediately contribute an additional PP this round.
Only a slight squint of concentration betrays the effort involved in keeping up the projection, and you keep control this round.
The ease with which you control the projection surprises even you. You do not need to contribute any PP the following round in order to continue. Are you wearing a pointy hat?

name: Science Maneuvers
notes: Table 3-20. Science category maneuver, used by the Architecture, Astronomy, Engineering, and Mathematics skills. UM 66 = Unusual Event on an unmodified roll of 66.
rows: Pitiful. You not only fail to produce accurate results, you produce wrong results. You cannot discover where you made your mistake and get -25 to this skill (until an Absolute Success is achieved).
You fail to grasp the problem and any time you have spent is wasted. You may not attempt again until you have reflected on this and 24 hours have passed.

Good heavens! In the course of your analysis, you have stumbled across a hitherto unrealized theorem! Abandoning your current task for several minutes you follow this line of reasoning instead of your original task. The world must be told of your revelation!
Persistence yields results. So persist. You may try the maneuver again (requiring the normal time) and get +10 to the roll.
You have blinded them with science! You have arrived at what you are sure is the right answer. Pretty sure. No, it's right. Almost certainly right.
Brilliant! Your seldom-appreciated genius rises to heretofore unseen levels. In half the time normally required, you manage to hold a command of the material that gives +10 on your next analysis.

name: Influence Maneuvers
notes: Table 3-21. Social category maneuver, used by the Influence skill (Charm, Duping, Intimidation), Leadership, Social Awareness, and Trading. UM 66 = Unusual Event on an unmodified roll of 66.
rows: Your clumsy attempts at manipulation are transparent to all, including the target who reacts accordingly. Future attempts to influence this target automatically fail (until an Absolute Success is achieved).
You fail to influence your target in the least. Your target may make a Social Awareness maneuver to note your attempt.

Your slick manner and glib tongue are utterly fascinating to your target. With Success, he will become your biggest fan to the point of annoyance. With Failure, he names you his arch-nemesis. Either way you will really need to get rid of this person.
You were doing great until you got impatient. You cut to the chase too soon, and your target is wary. If appropriate, roll again next round with a -10.
Oh ye of the silver tongue! You smooth-talk your target into tractability, and get the desired result.

You must have been an incubus in a former life. In any case, you have your target wrapped around your little finger and receive +30 to any future Influence attempts versus this target (until an Absolute Failure is achieved).

name: Technical Maneuvers
notes: Table 3-24. Technical category maneuver, used by the Locks, Mechanics, and Traps skills. UM 66 = Unusual Event on an unmodified roll of 66.
rows: You manage to ruin a tool of your trade through carelessness and frustration, and get -25 to all future uses of this skill (until an Absolute Success is achieved). Is this your idea of fun?
Your attempt fails utterly and you are dejected. If this is a trap there is a 20% it will activate, affecting you. You may not try this problem again for 24 hours.

As you are working on it, the mechanism activates, but incompletely. You have both hands occupied in the mechanism and if you let go with either hand it will activate or otherwise damage you. Hopefully, you brought a friend…
Unusual complications have arisen. Nothing you can't handle, but it's going to take some extra time. You may attempt to finish your work at -10.
You perform your task with admirable skill. Now quit patting yourself on the back and get back to work!
Your subtle technical mastery is reflected in your remarkable success. You may perform this specific maneuver at a +20 (non-cumulative) until you receive an Absolute Failure.

name: Level Spread
notes: Defines the Level spread for an encountered creature group — how much Levels may differ within the group. Each creature entry lists a spread code (A–H). Roll open-ended d100 and apply the listed modifier to the creature's base Level. "Young" indicates a juvenile/underdeveloped specimen. Only a guide: GMs may roll per creature, pick a Level from the spread, or ignore it entirely.

name: Outlook Types
notes: Outlook (demeanor) indicates how a creature reacts to outside influences and broadly shapes which Archetype it develops. Each creature entry lists one Outlook; the suggested Archetype(s) appear in each description. GMs may reassign an Outlook to suit a specific NPC (e.g. a more serious, Inquisitive pixie rather than the species-default Playful).
rows: Actively looking to commit violent acts. Provokes others, imposes itself on more timid beings and reacts forcefully to any provocation. Usually the Offensive Archetype; sometimes Stealth.

Aloofness with good reason: the being can easily escape, or is so powerful others pose no threat. Interaction is useless because it doesn't care, or it only checks whether violence is imminent. Defensive, or perhaps Spell caster Archetype.

Rare, going against survival of the fittest. The altruistic are rich beyond dreams, or have abilities freeing them from normal needs, so they help others even against their own goals or wealth. Discipline Archetype fits, though Hybrid or Spell user may be used.
Actively seeking to impose its will on others by any means, often violence. Attacks without provocation, or reacts badly to most interactions. Offensive Archetype, or Stealth.
Mindless or driven insane; indiscriminately attacks anything it perceives as living or a threat. Offensive Archetype is typical, as no other Archetype's abilities will be used.
A sign of control: convinced of its own superiority, imagined or real. Still reacts to interactions with appropriate measures. Discipline Archetypes, or Skill Archetypes in their normal environment.
Helps others and believes in the greater good of all. Positive and helpful; resorts to violence only if provoked repeatedly. Skill Archetypes, and Semi spell user Archetypes.

Out to get something big done. Feels everyone is involved and blatantly expects aid and alliance, taking it as a right if refused. Hard to reason with unless its goal parallels one's own. Discipline or Semi spell user Archetypes.
Most rotten: not only seeks violence but delights in inflicting pain, suffering and sadness. Stealth Archetypes fit, but Offensive Archetypes may be cruel as well.
Seeks to bend others to its will. Threats of violence or magic powers are frequent to cow others, and may be carried out for real. Spell users of any type, but also Offensive Archetypes.
Seeks to befriend beings without ulterior motive, desiring only companionship and talk. Spell user Archetypes, or Defensive ones.
Covets what it has not and may resort to any trick to get it - barter, stealing, or outright murder. Stealth Archetype is most appropriate, but also Skill.
All about helping others, but within reason, and usually has its own goals coinciding with those it aids. Skill Archetype, or Spell user Archetype.

Displays some demeanor other than its actual one, to mislead or trick others, or to escape persecution. Pursues its agenda regardless of the situation. Stealth Archetypes typically, but Spell users of any kind could qualify.
Very dangerous: violence ensues on any interaction. Differs from Berserk only in that it is not a state of mindless slaughter. Offensive Archetype only.
Has a normal disposition, but when hungry turns into something else and will hunt down anything edible. Offensive, or Skill Archetypes.
Looking for something on the move - game, or criminals. Asks questions or examines its surroundings, then leaves unless it has found its quarry. Stealth and Semi spell user Archetypes.
More a way of living: trying to get on top by any means. Does not care about the suffering of others and will tread down anything in its path. Stealth Archetypes.
Actively looking to learn more, whatever its motive. Intrudes on any business, private or not, and initiates interaction at every opportunity. Skill Archetypes, perhaps Semi spell users.
Extensively scans its surroundings; anything that startles or seems threatening makes it turn tail and bolt. Mostly Defensive Archetypes; Skill Archetypes may react the same.
Neither bolts at the first sign of danger nor charges headlong into every fight, but somewhere in between. Most beings leading an ordinary life. Skill and Discipline Archetypes fit well.
On a quest and intensely focused. Hindering it may lead to violence, helping it may earn friendship. Can talk of little else and distrusts distractions. Discipline and Semi spell user Archetypes.
About the exchange of information: doesn't mind questions, asked and asking. Can be too inquisitive but won't press on if rebuffed. Discipline and Semi spell user Archetypes.
Just waits to see what happens, not reacting fully to outside influences. Reacts against violence, but little else moves it. Sometimes Defensive Archetypes, and Spell user Archetypes.
Normally youthful or fey; wants fun all the time. For others its play may be serious - interactions are fun, embarrassing, or painful and may turn hostile. Spell user and Semi spell user Archetypes.

Has something valued it wants to protect - under a geas to stand watch, or a mother guarding her young. Approaching or taking the protected subject leads to violence. Defensive Archetypes, or Offensive.

Has a secret it must hide and will do anything, including posing as another demeanor, to keep it. The deception is always there. Spell user Archetypes are notoriously secretive, as are Stealth Archetypes.
Shy and unforthcoming; shies away from interaction, responds mostly with silence and backs off at every opportunity, especially against violence. Defensive Archetypes, and Skill Archetypes.

name: Size Examples
notes: Comparative creature size. Medium (the basic size) is roughly 6' tall and about 200 lbs. Weight (in lbs) determines a creature's size category. Larger creatures use larger attacks and may gain bulk and damage bonuses; smaller creatures hide more easily but suffer penalties in Feats of Strength, cannot use larger items, and take more damage from larger attacks. The Code column gives the size abbreviation and number. See Size Difference Adjustments (Table 3.1d) for combat effects.
rows: Triceratops, mature drake, mighty earth elemental

name: Size Difference Adjustments
notes: Critical severity is modified by the size difference between the attack and its target. Apply the Attack column adjustment based on the size of the attack, and the Defender column adjustment based on the defender's size. A larger attack raises critical severity; a larger defender lowers it. Beyond Enormous, continue the pattern at +1 per size category larger for the attack and -1 per size category larger for the defender.

name: Critical Codes
notes: A creature's Critical Code (Crit) starts with a roman numeral giving its size for critical purposes (V = Medium); compare the attack's size against it on the Size Difference Adjustments table to modify critical severity. Additional codes below modify how the creature suffers criticals. Example: "VII, @#" = suffers attacks as a Large (Size 7) creature, immune to stun (@) and bleeding (#).
rows: Creature reduces the severity of any critical by 1. This is in addition to any critical modifications due to Size difference.
This creature cannot be staggered, knocked back, or knocked down, due to its construction, physical make up, or a special ability.
Creature is immune to stun results. It suffers only a -25 penalty for the remainder of the round (next round if it has no Action Points left), not cumulative with other such results.
Creature is immune to bleeding results. Convert every hit of bleeding to a concussion hit.
No Grapple: This creature cannot be grappled. It takes half damage from grappling and wrestling attacks, and is unaffected by Grapple criticals.
No Krush: Creature takes half damage from crushing types of attacks, and is unaffected by Krush criticals.
No Punctures: Creature takes half damage from puncturing attacks, and is unaffected by Puncture criticals.
No Slash: Creature takes half damage from slashing attacks, and is unaffected by Slash criticals.
No Strikes: Creature takes half damage from unarmed strikes attacks, and is unaffected by Strikes criticals.
No Unbalancing: Creature takes half damage from unbalancing attacks, and is unaffected by Unbalancing criticals.

name: Attack Designations — Attack Size
notes: From Table 3.1f. The first part of an attack code is the size of the attack. Natural attacks list the size letter capitalized first; weapons and spell attacks give the size letter with the size class number in parentheses, then the attack-table abbreviation (e.g. "S(4) bs"). Compare the attack size against the target's Crit size code to set critical severity.

name: Attack Designations — Natural/Magical Attacks
notes: From Table 3.1f. Two- or three-letter codes for natural and magical (bolt/ball) attacks. * Crush and Trample attacks have size restrictions — see Core Law 7.1 and the optional rules under Attack Sequence Additions. A [crit type] note after an attack means a critical scored also applies an extra critical of that type, using the same critical roll.

name: Attack Designations — Weapon Attacks
notes: From Table 3.1f. Two-letter abbreviations for the weapon attack table a creature uses. Weapon and spell attacks are written with the size letter and size class number in parentheses first, then this abbreviation (e.g. "M(5) bs").

Climate guidelines: Cold = below-freezing temperatures the norm for more than 30% of the year; Tropical = almost never freezes; Temperate = in between. These are loose guidelines the GM may adjust. A lowercase "w" after an upper-case Biome code indicates a body of water or water-based area (lake, oasis, river, shoreline, spring, swamp, etc.) within that biome.

name: Armor Type Talent Summary
notes: From Table 3.2a. Summarizes the Natural Armor Type (AT) a creature's hide or carapace provides, with example Animal, Fantasy, and Manufactured-equivalent descriptions for each AT and the DP (Development Point) cost of the matching Natural Armor talent. AT 1 represents naked skin or light clothing. A creature with natural armor that also wears manufactured armor uses only the better of the two AT values (or the Piecemeal Armor rules if the worn armor covers only part of the body).
rows: Naked skin, light fur, light feather coat
Light blubber, heavy fur, thick feather coat, thick skin
Herbaceous fibrous structure, thick blubber, scaly bumps, small scales, thick leather skin
brittle, soft crystalline, Chitin, half plate
Arborescent fibrous structure, keratin laminae, small bone plates, large leather plates, leathery shell
chitin, full plate, very light dragon scales
Bone plate, keratin plate, rigid leathery shell
Brittle crystalline structure, thick calcium shell
light dragon scales, iron-like scales, giant feather coat, giant snail shell
Medium dragon scales, iron-like fur, iron-like flexible skin
heavy dragon scales, iron-like large plates, stone slab growths
crystaline skin, hardened chitin full plate

name: Material Density
notes: From Table 3.2b. Density of common materials in lbs per cubic foot, used to recalculate a creature's weight when it is made of a different material. Work out the weight as if it were flesh, then multiply by the material's density relative to flesh (Flesh = 63.1 lbs/cu.ft., slightly above water). Wood values are rough estimates for dry wood (12% moisture); wet or "green" wood can be up to ~16% heavier. Add 10% per size category above Medium for morphological scaling. Hollow mechanical creatures may weigh 10-50% less (GM discretion).

name: Avoidance Roll Modifiers
notes: From Table 15.4a. Modifiers applied to the open-ended d100 Avoidance Roll made by a player. Compare against the GM's Activity Roll: the Encounter Differential = Adjusted Activity Roll − Adjusted Avoidance Roll. If the differential is below 1 the encounter is avoided (and any pursuit loses the trail); if positive, consult the Standard Encounters table. * Stealth skill applies whenever the party is trying to avoid encounters (use the best skill value, minus the ranks of the others).
rows: + best skill value (minus ranks of others)
Traveling at night (light, moon, etc. = variable)

name: Activity Roll Modifiers
notes: From Table 15.4a. Modifiers applied to the open-ended d100 Activity Roll made by the GM. Compare against the player's Avoidance Roll: the Encounter Differential = Adjusted Activity Roll − Adjusted Avoidance Roll. If positive, consult the Standard Encounters table. * Tracking skill (patrolled area) uses the best skill value, minus the ranks of the others.
rows: + best skill value (minus ranks of others)

name: Standard Encounters
notes: From Table 15.4b. Cross-reference the Encounter Differential (see Encounter Modifiers) against the population density of the area. Each cell is "social inhabitants encounter / creature encounter". First letter (social): a = attacking encounter, b = basic encounter, h = hostile encounter, s = sighting encounter, t = trap/ambush encounter. Second letter (creature): a = party is attacked, b = encounter is neutral, h = encounter is hostile, s = encounter sighted at a distance, t = party is ambushed or runs into a trap; -- = no encounter. If a roll exceeds 300 or is below 01 the GM may use the extreme result or "roll over". Any unmodified roll of 66 indicates a Special situation (see Special Encounter Situations).

name: Special Encounter Situations
notes: From Table 15.4c. Any unmodified roll of 66 on the encounter tables indicates a Special situation or occurrence (per the Rolemaster dice mechanics). It is up to the GM to decide what it is; the following are suggestions by biome.
rows: Nest of a strange beast, or a cave with runes
Changeling, doppelganger, spy/assassin, or a similar situation that is not as it seems
Extra-dimensional portal, magical runic standing stones, strange phenomenon
Enchanted clearing or grove, fairy hideaway, enchanted spring
Ruined and half-buried citadel / city / camp, strange phenomenon
Extra-dimensional portal, another adventuring party, strange phenomenon
Eruption, earthquake, another adventuring party, strange phenomenon
